modelStrategy.why: Mechanical work — copy templates, create files, write structured data

modelStrategy.taskType: Simple search, file reading, basic edits
modelStrategy.why: Fast lookups that don't need reasoning
Good balance of speed and quality for most coding tasks
Handles complexity without the cost of Opus
Needs deep judgment, multi-step reasoning, and nuanced understanding
Creative and strategic thinking — where quality matters most

agentPatterns.name: Solo Agent
agentPatterns.description: One task, one agent. The simplest pattern — dispatch a single agent to handle an isolated piece of work.
agentPatterns.whenToUse: For simple, self-contained tasks that don't interact with other work. Scripts, config files, single components.
agentPatterns.example: DevToolkit Task 12 (mobile polish) ran as a solo agent — it touched multiple files but was one cohesive task.

agentPatterns.name: Parallel Dispatch
agentPatterns.description: Multiple independent tasks running simultaneously. Each agent works on its own files without conflicts.
agentPatterns.whenToUse: When you have 2+ tasks that don't share files or state. Pages, content files, independent components.
agentPatterns.diagram:
Task A → Agent A ──┐
Task B → Agent B ──┼→ All Done
Task C → Agent C ──┘
agentPatterns.example: DevToolkit Tasks 2, 3, 4 ran in parallel — types, tool JSON files, and learning path content. No conflicts because they touched different directories.

agentPatterns.name: Sequential with Review
agentPatterns.description: Implement → spec review → quality review. Each task goes through two review gates before moving on.
agentPatterns.whenToUse: For quality-critical work where mistakes are costly. Auth systems, payment integration, data migrations.
agentPatterns.diagram: Implement → Spec Review → Quality Review → Next Task
agentPatterns.example: Each DevToolkit page task was implemented by Sonnet, then reviewed by Opus for spec compliance and code quality.

agentPatterns.name: Escalation
agentPatterns.description: Start with a cheaper model. If it gets stuck or produces poor results, re-dispatch with a more capable model.
agentPatterns.whenToUse: When you're unsure about task complexity. Start cheap, upgrade only if needed.
agentPatterns.diagram: Task → Haiku/Sonnet → Blocked? → Re-dispatch with Opus → Done
agentPatterns.example: If a Sonnet agent reported BLOCKED on a complex debugging task, you'd re-dispatch the same task with Opus for deeper reasoning.

tokenEfficiency: Match model to task complexity — don't use Opus to create JSON files when Haiku does it perfectly
Never send full conversation history to subagents — craft precise, focused prompts with only the context they need
Parallelize independent tasks to save wall-clock time — 3 parallel agents finish faster than 3 sequential ones
Fresh agent per task prevents context pollution — each agent starts clean without irrelevant prior context
Use the cheapest model that handles the task well — not just 'handles' but 'handles well'. Quality still matters.

realExample.title: How DevToolkit Was Built
realExample.description: 14 tasks completed in one session using subagent-driven development with parallel batches.

batches.tasks: Tasks 2, 3, 4: Types, tool content, learning path
